interpretation: Your sugar over the past 2–3 months is in a very healthy range — keep your current diet and routine to maintain this.
Your long-term blood sugar is normal — good control; continue healthy eating, activity, and regular checkups.
Your HbA1c is at the upper end of normal — monitor regularly and maintain healthy habits to prevent progression.
This range shows early signs of pre-diabetes — small lifestyle changes now can help reverse it.
Your HbA1c indicates established pre-diabetes — stronger lifestyle changes and regular monitoring are recommended.
This HbA1c meets the diagnostic cutoff for diabetes — please discuss confirmation and next steps with your doctor.
Your HbA1c suggests moderate diabetes — review medications and lifestyle to prevent complications.
Your HbA1c indicates poor diabetic control — sugar levels need adjustment to avoid long-term risks.
Your long-term sugar level is very high — this increases risk of nerve, kidney, eye and heart issues; medical review needed.
Extremely high HbA1c — your risk of severe complications is very high; urgent medical care is required.
Your fasting sugar is dangerously low — this can cause fainting or confusion; take fast sugar and get urgent medical help.
Your fasting sugar is low — monitor symptoms, adjust food/medication timing and review with your doctor if recurrent.
Your fasting sugar is excellent — shows healthy overnight glucose control; keep maintaining your habits.
Your fasting sugar is normal — good glucose regulation; continue your routine and regular monitoring.
Your fasting sugar is slightly high — monitor diet/activity and recheck to prevent further rise.
Early fasting pre-diabetes — your body is beginning to struggle with sugar control; lifestyle action now helps.
Established fasting pre-diabetes — consistent elevation; diet, exercise and monitoring strongly recommended.
Fasting sugar meets diabetes criteria — follow up with your doctor for diagnosis and management.
Moderately elevated fasting sugar — poor control; medical and lifestyle adjustments are recommended.
Severely elevated fasting sugar — high risk of long-term complications; seek medical care soon.
Your post-meal sugar is dangerously low — take fast sugar and seek urgent medical evaluation.
Your post-meal sugar is low — may be from delayed meals/medication imbalance; monitor closely.
Your post-meal sugar is optimal — strong metabolic control; maintain your habits.
Your post-meal sugar is normal — good glucose handling after meals.
Your post-meal sugar is slightly high — early warning sign; adjust food portions and meal habits.
Early post-meal pre-diabetes — some difficulty controlling sugar after meals; improve diet/lifestyle.
Established post-meal pre-diabetes — sugar stays high after eating; lifestyle corrections needed.
Your post-meal sugar meets diabetes criteria — please consult your doctor.
Moderately elevated post-meal sugar — poor control; treatment and lifestyle changes advised.
Severely elevated post-meal sugar — significantly increases complication risk; immediate care advised.
Your average glucose is dangerously low — indicates frequent lows; urgent medical evaluation required.
Your average glucose is low — repeated mild lows possible; review medication/meal habits.
Your average glucose is optimal — excellent overall sugar control.
Your average glucose is normal — healthy metabolic balance.
Your average glucose is slightly high — early sign of impaired glucose control; lifestyle changes help.
Early pre-diabetic average glucose — insulin resistance may be starting; take preventive action.
Established pre-diabetic average glucose — consistent elevation; lifestyle correction required.
Your average glucose crosses diabetes cutoff — consult your doctor.
Poor average glucose control — sustained high levels raise complication risk; treatment review needed.
Severely elevated average glucose — uncontrolled diabetes; urgent medical care required.
TSH extremely low — strong hyperthyroid activity; urgent assessment recommended.
TSH low — possible hyperthyroidism; review symptoms with your doctor.
TSH normal — healthy thyroid hormone balance.
TSH slightly high — early hypothyroidism; monitoring advised.
TSH high — moderate hypothyroidism; treatment may be needed.
TSH extremely high — severe hypothyroidism; immediate care advised.
Free T3 critically low — severe thyroid underactivity; seek evaluation.
Free T3 low — may indicate hypothyroidism or low hormone conversion.
Free T3 normal — healthy active thyroid hormone levels.
Free T3 high — possible hyperthyroidism; review with your doctor.
Free T3 very high — strong thyroid overactivity; urgent evaluation advised.
Free T4 critically low — severe hypothyroidism; immediate medical review needed.
Free T4 low — underactive thyroid suggested; discuss treatment.
Free T4 normal — healthy thyroid hormone levels.
Free T4 high — possible hyperthyroidism or medication overdose.
Free T4 very high — severe hyperthyroidism; urgent assessment required.
Hemoglobin critically low — severe anemia; urgent medical care needed.
Hemoglobin low — anemia likely; may cause fatigue and weakness.
Hemoglobin normal — healthy oxygen-carrying capacity.
Hemoglobin high — may occur from dehydration or increased red cell production.
Hemoglobin very high — possible blood disorder or chronic low oxygen; evaluation advised.
RBC critically low — severe anemia or bone marrow issue; urgent care needed.
RBC low — may indicate anemia or nutrient deficiency.
RBC normal — healthy red cell count.
RBC high — may be due to dehydration or increased red cell production.
RBC very high — may suggest polycythemia or chronic hypoxia; evaluation needed.
WBC critically low — high infection risk; immune suppression suspected.
WBC low — may indicate viral infection or bone marrow issues.
WBC normal — healthy immune function.
WBC high — commonly due to infection or inflammation.
WBC very high — severe infection or blood disorder suspected.
Platelets critically low — high bleeding risk; urgent care advised.
Platelets low — may cause easy bruising or bleeding.
Platelets normal — healthy clotting function.
Platelets high — may occur with inflammation or reactive conditions.
Platelets very high — possible bone marrow disorder; evaluation needed.
MCV critically low — severe iron deficiency or microcytic anemia.
MCV low — common in iron deficiency or thalassemia.
MCV normal — normal red cell size.
MCV high — often due to B12 or folate deficiency.
MCV very high — significant macrocytosis; possible marrow disorder.
MCH critically low — severe hypochromic anemia.
MCH low — common in iron deficiency.
MCH normal — healthy hemoglobin content per red cell.
MCH high — often linked to macrocytic anemia.
MCH very high — abnormal RBC morphology suspected.
MCHC critically low — severe iron deficiency or hypochromia.
MCHC low — slightly low hemoglobin concentration.
MCHC normal — healthy hemoglobin concentration.
MCHC high — may occur in spherocytosis or dehydration.
MCHC very high — possible RBC membrane disorders.
RDW low — red blood cells are unusually uniform.
RDW normal — consistent RBC size distribution.
RDW high — often seen in iron deficiency or mixed anemia.
RDW very high — large variation in RBC size; further evaluation needed.
Neutrophils very low — severe infection risk.
Neutrophils low — may be due to viral infection or marrow suppression.
Neutrophils normal — healthy immune response.
Neutrophils high — suggests bacterial infection or inflammation.
Neutrophils very high — severe infection or stress response.
Lymphocytes very low — high infection susceptibility.
Lymphocytes low — may occur during acute infection or stress.
Lymphocytes normal — healthy immune function.
Lymphocytes high — may indicate viral infection or inflammation.
Lymphocytes very high — may suggest lymphoproliferative disorders.
